chunk_summary: Counsel-first is preferred for growth faltering in stable children aged 6–24 months, with targeted testing reserved for red flags or lack of response to feeding interventions.
learning_objectives: Understand the criteria for using a counsel-first approach in growth faltering for children aged 6–24 months.
Identify red flags that necessitate targeted testing in growth faltering cases.
Learn practical first-line tests for evaluating growth faltering when red flags are present.
Recognize modifiable feeding domains contributing to poor weight gain.
key_facts: Counsel-first is appropriate for clinically stable children with age-appropriate development and clear dietary risks.
Structured 24-hour bowl recall and meal observation can guide feeding interventions; recheck weight in 2 weeks.
Red flags include pallor, pica, chronic diarrhea, organomegaly, developmental regression, and coughing/choking during feeds.
Targeted tests include CBC with indices, urinalysis/culture, celiac screening, and thyroid testing based on symptoms.
Improved weight velocity after 2 weeks supports inadequate intake or feeding interaction as the main cause.
reference_quote: ‘Move early to “test-first” evaluation when red flags suggest disease or when there is no weight response after 2–4 weeks of correctly implemented feeding changes.’ (Source: NICE NG75)
document_key_topics: Complementary feeding in children aged 6–24 months
Targeted testing for red flags in growth faltering
Modifiable feeding domains and their impact on weight gain
